title: God Spatially Above and Spatially Extended
content:
## Introduction

### Purpose
Hoover argues that Ibn Taymiyya rationally defends divine spatiality rather than merely asserting scriptural literalism. Bayān Talbīs al-Jahmiyyah (BTJ) is presented as a direct refutation of Fakhr al-Dīn al-Rāzī's Taʾsīs al-Taqdīs and arguably the most extensive anti-kalām work in the Islamic tradition.

## Historical Context

### Fakhr al-Dīn al-Rāzī
• Taʾsīs al-Taqdīs written around 596 AH.
• Represents mature Ashʿarī incorporealism.

### Ibn Taymiyya
• Bayān Talbīs al-Jahmiyyah written in Egypt (c. 705–712 AH).
• Composed after the Ḥamawiyyah controversy and during his imprisonment.
• Intended primarily as a response to al-Rāzī.

## Four Positions on Divine Spatiality

1. Noncognitivism
• Representative: Ibn Qudāmah.
• Affirms scriptural texts without interpretation (bilā kayf).

2. Corporealism
• Representative: Karrāmiyyah.
• Explicitly identifies God as a body (jism).

3. Spatialism
• Representative: al-Dārimī.
• God is above creation without necessarily being called a body.
• Hoover places Ibn Taymiyya in this category.

4. Incorporealism
• Representatives: Muʿtazilah and Ashʿarīs.
• God is neither body nor place.
• Divine attributes interpreted figuratively (taʾwīl).

## al-Rāzī's Argument

• Body implies composition.
• Composition implies contingency.
• Place implies dependence.
• Direction implies limitation.
• Therefore God is neither body nor located in a place.

## Ibn Taymiyya's Reply

• Rejects al-Rāzī's definitions before addressing his conclusions.
• 'Body', 'place', and 'direction' are ambiguous technical terms.
• Meanings must be distinguished individually.
• Affirm what is true.
• Reject what is false.
• Revelation and sound reason ultimately agree.

## Ibn Rushd's Influence

Hoover argues that Ibn Taymiyya selectively adopts Ibn Rushd's Aristotelian definition of place as the inner surface of the containing body. This concept is used to undermine al-Rāzī's argument without accepting Aristotelian philosophy as a whole.

## Structure of Ibn Taymiyya's Argument

al-Rāzī's premise → Question the definition → Expose equivocation → Distinguish meanings → Appeal to Qurʾān and Sunnah → Provide rational support → Conclusion.

## Comparison with Ibn Rushd

### Similarities
• Reject kalām technical definitions.
• Employ the Aristotelian concept of place.
• Criticize unsound demonstrations.

### Differences
• Ibn Rushd prioritizes philosophy.
• Ibn Taymiyya subordinates reason to revelation, affirms scriptural attributes, and rejects philosophers where they conflict with revelation.

## Key Takeaways

• Body does not necessarily imply divisibility.
• Spatial relation does not necessarily imply containment.
• Divine aboveness does not entail anthropomorphism.
• Technical terminology must be distinguished from ordinary linguistic usage.

## References

Good quotations: p. xx
Important references: p. xx

title: Defining without Essences: Ibn Taymiyya’s Critique of Aristotelian Logic
